texto: I wou'd therefore exhort all my sex (...) to betake themselves to the improvement of their minds (...) and (...) shew our selves worthy something from them, as much above their bare esteem, as they conceit themselves above us. In a word, let us shew them, by what little we do without aid of education, the much we might do if they did us justice; that we may force a blush from them, if possible, and compel them to confess their own baseness to us, and that the worst of us deserve much better treatment than the best of us receive.
explicacao: Citação de Lady Sophia Fermor sobre temas ligados a autoestima. “I wou'd therefore exhort all my sex (...) to betake themselves to the improvement of their minds …” articula autoestima, 18th-century, 18th-century-feminism em registro de autoestima, com relevância para contextos como 18th-century-feminism e feminism.

texto: There will be great reason to suspect the Men of jealousy; and it cannot be rash to say, that their only reason for locking up from us all the avenues of knowledge, is the fear of our excelling them in it. (...) Had we the same advantages of study allowed us which the Men have, there is no room to doubt but we should at least keep pace with them in the sciences, and every useful knowledge. It can only then be a mean dastardly jealousy in them to exclude us from those advantages, in which we have so natural a right to emulate them.
explicacao: Orientação direta de Lady Sophia Fermor sobre limites e escolhas conscientes. texto: the many absurd notions the Men are led into by custom: Tho' there is none more absurd, than that of the great difference they make between their own sex and ours. Yet it must be own'd that there is not any vulgar error more antient or universal. For the learned and illiterate alike are prepossest with the opinion that Men are really superior to Women, and that the dependence we now are in, is the very state which nature pointed out for us.

texto: Where is there a Woman, who having generously trusted her liberty with a husband, does not immediately find the spaniel metamorphosed into a tyger, or has not reason to envy the lesser misery of a bond-slave to a merciless tyrant?

texto: And the differences thence arising [between the constitution of men and women] are no ways sufficient to argue more natural strength in the one than in the other, to qualify them more for military labours. Are not the Women of different degrees of strength, like the Men? Are there not strong and weak of both sexes? Men educated in sloth and softness are weaker than Women; and Women, become harden'd by necessity, are often more robust than Men. (...) Woman may be enured to all the hardships of a campaign, and to meet all the terrors of it, as well as the bravest of the opposite sex.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Lady Sophia Fermor: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de temas ligados a coragem. O trecho “And the differences thence arising [between the constitution of men and women] are no ways suffic…” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que coragem, 18th-century, 18th-century-feminism pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de 18th-century-feminism e amazon-feminism.

texto: I think it evidently appears, that there is no science, office, or dignity, which Women have not an equal right to share in with the Men: Since there can be no superiority, but that of brutal strength, shewn in the latter, to entitle them to engross all power and prerogative to themselves: nor any incapacity proved in the former, to disqualify them of their right, but what is owing to the unjust oppression of the Men, and might be easily removed.

texto: What has greatly help'd to confirm the Men in the prejudiced notion of Women's natural weakness, is the common manner of expression which this very vulgar error gave birth to. When they mean to stigmatise a Man with want of courage they call him effeminate, and when they would praise a Woman for her courage they call her manly. But as these, and such like expressions, are merely arbitrary and but a fulsome compliment which the Men pass on themselves, they establish no truth.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Lady Sophia Fermor: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de enfrentar incertezas com ação. O trecho “What has greatly help'd to confirm the Men in the prejudiced notion of Women's natural weakness, …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, 18th-century, 18th-century-feminism pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de termino e 18th-century-feminism.

texto: The Men who cannot deny us to be rational creatures, wou'd have us justify their irrational opinion and treatment of us, by descending to a mean compliance with their irrational Expectations. But I hope, while Women have any spirit left, they will exert it all, in shewing how worthy they are of better usage, by not submitting tamely to such misplaced arrogance.
